title: The Weyl law for algebraic tori
speaker: Ian Petrow
abstract: A basic but difficult question in the analytic theory of automorphic forms is: given a reductive group G and a representation r of its L-group, how many automorphic representations of bounded analytic conductor are there? In this talk I will present an answer to this question in the case that G is a torus over a number field.

proof_stages.label: Specialization to automorphic characters of a torus
proof_stages.summary: The broad question of counting automorphic representations of bounded analytic conductor is specialized to continuous unitary automorphic characters of an algebraic torus T, with a Haar measure fixed and a finite-dimensional algebraic representation r of the L-group LT chosen.

proof_stages.label: Analytic conductor through local Langlands for tori
proof_stages.summary: The global r-analytic conductor of an automorphic character is defined as a product of local conductors by passing through the local Langlands correspondence for tori, composing the resulting parameter with r, and using Artin conductors at finite places and analytic conductors at Archimedean places.

proof_stages.label: Co-weight combinatorics and the main Weyl law
proof_stages.summary: The restriction of r to the dual torus is encoded by its multiset of co-weights, the diagonalizable subgroups D(S) are defined from subsets of co-weights, and under faithfulness the exponent a=max_S (dim D(S)+1)/|S| enters the asymptotic count x^a P(log x)+O(x^δ).

proof_stages.label: Galois-orbit formula for the logarithmic degree
proof_stages.summary: To determine deg P, the speaker introduces the Galois action on max-attaining subsets S, the component-group power action after adjoining roots of unity, the fibered set of pairs (S,y), and states that deg P is the number of resulting Galois orbits minus one.

proof_stages.label: Generating series criterion for the asymptotic
proof_stages.summary: The counting theorem is reduced to analytic properties of the Dirichlet-type generating integral over automorphic characters, namely absolute convergence for Re(s)>a, meromorphic continuation past the line Re(s)=a, and a pole at s=a whose order controls the logarithmic polynomial.

proof_stages.label: Poisson summation and adelic factorization
proof_stages.summary: After choosing local norm images at finite places, Poisson summation converts the global generating series into a sum over modified global units of products of Archimedean integrals and finite local character sums.

proof_stages.label: Unramified finite-place conductor computation
proof_stages.summary: At unramified finite places the norm map is surjective, the local Langlands correspondence identifies characters of integral points with cohomological parameters, and the local conductor decomposes in terms of the one-dimensional characters obtained by composing those parameters with the co-weights μ_i.

proof_stages.label: Archimedean convergence by Brascamp-Lieb and matroid optimization
proof_stages.summary: The Archimedean integrals are controlled using a Brascamp-Lieb inequality for the integer matrix of co-weight exponents, with the remaining convergence region reduced to a combinatorial optimization problem related to matroids.

examples.name: Split real torus and its Archimedean conductor
examples.note: For a torus split over R, the speaker writes explicit Weil-group parameters, gamma factors, and the resulting local analytic conductor, making the abstract local definition concrete rather than deriving a new idea from it.
examples.engagement_reason: For the split real torus he writes the real Weil group explicitly, parametrizes the Langlands parameters, inserts the representation weights, and derives the archimedean L-factor and conductor formula.

passes.reason: A split real torus is the maximally split real form used to compute the Archimedean conductor, not to refute a claim.
the split real case has trivial local Galois action/L-group twisting and is used only to compute the Archimedean conductor

examples.name: G_m and classical Hecke characters
examples.note: Taking T=G_m and r the identity recovers counting primitive Hecke characters, including Dirichlet characters over Q together with |.|^{it}, and illustrates that the theorem gives the expected exponent a=2.
examples.engagement_reason: For T=G_m with the identity representation he identifies the counted objects as Hecke characters and computes the D(S) cases to get a=2 and the expected x^2 growth.

passes.reason: G_m is the minimal nontrivial split torus/base case giving classical Hecke characters.
G_m with the identity representation is the simplest one-dimensional split-torus base case giving classical Hecke characters
G_m with the identity representation is the minimal split-torus base case giving classical Hecke characters, not a counterexample.
G_m is the rank-one split torus, the minimal nontrivial and maximally split base case for Hecke characters.
G_m is the rank-one split torus, hence a maximally split boundary case, used to recover the classical Hecke-character count.
G_m is the minimal nontrivial split torus/base case giving classical Hecke characters, not used adversarially
G_m is the minimal basic split-torus case giving the classical Hecke-character count, not a failure case
G_m is used as the simplest Hecke-character case, and is the one-dimensional minimal/maximally split torus.
G_m is the rank-one split, minimal nontrivial and maximally split torus, used as the classical Hecke-character benchmark.
It is the simplest GL1/Hecke-character base case, a one-dimensional split torus with minimal nontrivial dimension and maximal split rank.
Used as the simplest illustrative case; G_m is the rank-one split torus, a minimal base case and maximally split.
G_m with the identity representation is the rank-one split-torus base case for classical Hecke characters.

examples.name: Norm-one torus of an extension L/F
examples.note: For T=Res^1_{L/F} G_m with the natural permutation representation on the dual torus, the computation dim D(S)=|S|-1 gives a=1 and later shows how the logarithmic power is governed by Galois orbits on subsets.
examples.engagement_reason: For the norm-one torus he writes the dual torus, Galois action and representation, identifies the co-weights, computes dim D(S)=|S|-1 and a=1, and later derives the log-power behavior for Galois groups.

passes.reason: The norm-one torus Res^1_{L/F} G_m is a standard infinite-family torus used as an illustrative example.

Used to illustrate the theorem and log-degree behavior; a norm-one torus of a field extension is a standard anisotropic/minimal-split-rank family, not sporadic.
The norm-one torus is a standard infinite family and, for a field extension, is anisotropic with zero split rank, used as a nontrivial illustration.

examples.name: One-dimensional non-split torus via Hilbert 90
examples.note: In the quadratic norm-one case, Hilbert 90 identifies the automorphic characters as Hecke characters upstairs trivial on the lower-field ideles, clarifying what family is being counted under functorial/base-change language.
examples.engagement_reason: For the one-dimensional non-split torus he invokes Hilbert 90 to write an exact sequence and uses it to identify the resulting characters and their base-change description.
